responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Man as a Machine—Reflections on Certain Cybernetic Implications of Freud's Psychoanalytic Theories with Special Consideration of the Evolutionary Association of Love and Understanding (Knowing)

Robert Marcus M.D.

Robert Marcus

Robert L. Marcus, M.D.
50 West 96 Street
New York, N.Y. 10025

Robert Marcus  

SUMMARY

The mind is the control and regulatory system that governs the behavior of the human organism and as such is the sensorial manifestation of the somatic or bodily influences which direct this behavior. Cybernetics is a new scientific discipline concerned with the study of control and regulatory systems. While Freud's psychoanalytic techniques for studying the human mind appear to bear no resemblance to current cybernetic methodology, we must conclude that psychoanalytic concepts and data are in the final analysis the manifestation of cybernetic processes. One of the principal obstacles to the recognition by cyberneticists that psychoanalytic concepts are the proper concern of cybernetics is their persistent aversion to a recognition of the significance of instinct in general and love in particular. Psychoanalysts, on the other hand, appear concerned that the phenomenon of love will be lost in any study involving the machine science of cybernetics which to date has been unable to recognize that processes involving information and decision making are not the most characteristic features of human behavior.

Love is a prominent precipitate of biological evolution. As such, we have reason to presume, it represents a central feature in the regulatory and control processes that have accrued to man as a result of natural selection. Moreover, since regulation and control are in essence the subject matter of cybernetics, Freud's psychoanalytic theories and his theory of libido must harbor cybernetic principles which are of fundamental importance. One of these must be that in the human organism love is fundamentally associated with knowing and understanding. In essence, knowing and understanding are not possible in man without love. Knowing and understanding are intimately associated with the libidinal instinct to unify by contact or touch, and by the libidinal instinct to unify abstractly by being like or by becoming like (identification).

The association of love with knowledge is most clearly evident

419

in curiosity. However, we are reminded in this case of the importance of the aggressive or destructive instinct as a companion of the libidinal instinct.

We are also reminded of the fact that opposites play such an important part in mental processes and that in the unconscious the concept of negation does not exist (8). This may well represent an instance where psychoanalytic concepts have led the way for the physical scientist in their implication that some form of "logic" must exist wherein opposites are equivalent. That such is the case is suggested by the recent work of Gunther. He has concluded that formal classical logic of the excluded middle must represent a limiting case of a more general "transclassical" logic. One of the mathematical properties of this logic would be that under certain conditions the important property of the unconscious whereby opposites are equivalent would be characteristic of a complex system.

Many other examples could be given of the cybernetic implications of psychoanalytic discoveries. However, I shall note only one final one. The phenomenon of repression and the developmental process which functionally separates the system unconscious and the past from the system conscious and current reality are central and crucial to Freud's concept of mental functioning. This division which so many continue to view as metaphysical is readily understandable in physical terms with an understanding of Ashby's concepts of partially independent dynamic systems. The cybernetic study of such systems is only beginning, but the relationship between the psychoanalytic concepts and the cybernetic ones is readily apparent and deserves recognition by experts in both fields.

Machines are a creation of human curiosity and inventiveness, and their increasing complexity is a reflection of human creativity. Machines are being created which are progressively capable of performing many of man's tasks. This does not mean, however, that man is about to recreate himself out of nonhuman substances. In fact, the idea itself is fundamentally contradictory. On the other hand, there can be no doubt that in the development of such complex machines we are developing conceptual tools which will progressively contribute to our efforts to understand ourselves. Cybernetics

420

cannot acknowledge Freud's discoveries and at the same time attempt to supplant his concepts. His discoveries are an integral part of his concepts and would become an interesting but unrelated conglomeration of facts without his conceptual framework. Instead cybernetics must expand its own horizons and, as a beginning, must come to recognize the cybernetic implications of Freud's ideas or else give up any pretense of being concerned with the human mind or behavior. This may seem like an unrealistic task. But the concepts of Freud's psychoanalysis and his theory of instincts on the one hand and of cybernetics on the other are much more closely related than experts in either field are currently ready to recognize.

[[RunningHead]]

The Relation of Experimentally Induced Presleep Experiences to Dreams—A Report on Method and Preliminary Findings

Herman Witkin Ph.D. and Helen Lewis Ph.D.

Helen Lewis

Herman A. Witkin, Ph.D.
Helen B. Lewis, Ph.D.
450 Clarkson Avenue
Brooklyn, N.Y. 11203

Helen Lewis  

SUMMARY

A research procedure has been described for studying the uniquely personal way in which thoughts and feelings stirred in the waking state are represented in subsequent dreams.

The main feature of this procedure is to have the subject undergo an arousing experience just before he goes to sleep; this presleep event then serves as one reference point or "tracer element" for studying transformations. The presleep event we use consists, on some occasions, of an emotionally charged film; on other occasions (for comparison purposes) a neutral film; and on still other occasions, an encounter with another person through the medium of suggestion. The procedure has several other salient features. By means of a special technique the content of the subject's reverie between the presleep event and the time he falls asleep is obtained. The subject is awakened during all dream periods (identified by the concomitant occurrence of rapid eye movements and Stage-1 EEG) and reports his dreams. Recordings are made of autonomic activity during the presleep event and during sleep. At the end of the sleep session an extended inquiry is conducted into each dream, including associations to the dreams, and into the subject's reactions to the entire experience.

Each subject is intensively studied before participating in the experiment; additional knowledge is gained in the postsleep inquiry of each experimental session of the series; and this knowledge

846

is further supplemented by a biographical interview at the very end of the series of experimental sessions, in which dream associations are further pursued. In these various ways a rich clinical context is provided for studying the dreams.

Preliminary studies with this procedure have been carried out with a group of ten subjects, who participated in a total of thirty experimental sessions. Results observed thus far show that elements from the experimentally induced presleep event may be clearly identified in subsequent dreams, often symbolically transformed. The clinical information we are able to obtain about our subjects has made it possible to connect the way in which the presleep event is represented in the dreams of a particular subject with his experience of the total experimental situation and with significant events in his personal life.

The procedure that has been developed yields evidence on a number of problems of interest for dream theory. In addition to its value in studies of the relation between waking experience and the dream, the procedure is particularly useful for studying dream recall and forgetting; the presleep excitement may, in some persons, make subsequent dreams more likely candidates for repression and so reduce dream recall. The procedure may also be useful in exploring the relation between "style of dreaming" and differences in personality.

Remarks on Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness

Richard Sterba M.D.

Richard Sterba

Richard F. Sterba, M.D.
861 Whittier Boulevard
Grosse Point, Mich. 482303

Richard Sterba  

SUMMARY

In this paper Phyllis Greenacre's concept of collective (cosmic) alternates is applied to Joseph Conrad's well-known story Heart of Darkness. The paper attempts to demonstrate that the description of the African landscape is based on a projective-introjective experience of bodily Gestalten. The intense effect of these descriptions is based on the willingness to fuse self and outside world, which the author's linguistic skill evokes in the reader.

[[RunningHead]]

HEINZ HARTMANN - Seventieth Birthday

The American Psychoanalytic Association is proud indeed to dedicate this issue of the Joumal to Heinz Hartmann in honor of his seventieth birthday which took place on November 4, 1964. This occasion presents a most felicitous opportunity to reaffirm the deep sense of gratitude we owe to Heinz Hartmann for his contributions to psychoanalysis. It is superfluous to reemphasize these contributions, which have so vitally stimulated psychoanalytic thinking and will continue to do so for many years to come. Once before we expressed these thoughts when we initiated the Monograph series of the Joumal with the first English publication of Ego Psychology and the Problem of Adaptation. We are very glad to wish him "many happy retums" and hope to do so again for his eightieth birthday.

194

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and the Controversy Over Shakespearean Authorship

Harry Trosman M.D.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman, M.D.
950 East 59 Street
Chicago 37, Ill.

Harry Trosman  

SUMMARY

Freud maintained a long-standing fascination with the Shakespearean authorship controversy. After reading J. Thomas Looney's book, "Shakespeare" Identified, in 1923 he became "almost convinced" that Edward de Vere, the seventeenth Earl of Oxford, was the rightful author of the Shakespearean plays and poems.

The Looney book is examined as representative of the anti-Stratfordian

496

position. The arguments are compared with the position taken by the body of historical and literary scholars who support the "Stratfordian orthodoxy."

Lastly, an attempt is made to examine the controversy psychoanalytically. Some speculations concerning motivation of those who support the anti-Stratfordian position are put forth. Freud's interest in the controversy is seen as the outgrowth of a specific cognitive style and a variant of a family romance fantasy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heinz Hartmann—A Tribute

Anna Freud

Anna Freud

Anna Freud  

THE PIONEERS of psychoanalysis, among whom Heinz Hartmann is an outstanding figure, have been known at all times to wage war on more than one front. As uncompromising representatives of a new discipline, they inevitably find themselves in opposition to the academic and clinical departments in which they have grown up. As innovators and explorers in their chosen field, they cannot help incurring the distrust and misgivings of their new colleagues. If, like Hartmann, they simultaneously stand firm on all the valued and tested acquisitions of psychoanalysis, they expose themselves also to the reproach of "orthodoxy" from the side of the more extreme of the analytic revolutionaries. It is only on rare occasions, such as anniversary celebrations, that the dissenting voices recede into the background and make room for universal acclaim and an adequate evaluation of their role within the fabric of the analytic movement and the growth and development of analytic theory.

In spite of this precarious position at the top of a profession, Heinz Hartmann, in his teaching activities and publications, seems to have completely disregarded or even remained oblivious to the battle which was staged around him. In the place of polemics, criticism of fellow workers, or bids to win approval, there is only the author's deep immersion into the problems which engage his own interest and for which he tries to gain the students', the readers', or the audiences' attention.

The range and sequence of these topics and their bearing on the structure of psychoanalysis as a whole are well known by now.

195

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child—A Tribute

Twenty years ago there appeared the first issue of what has become one of the most highly respected and valued psychoanalytic publications—The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. From the very beginning its success was assured by the composition of the Editorial Board, a success maintained by the unvarying application of the highest scientific standards. The burden of the organizational and managerial duties, which first fell upon the shoulders of Anna Freud, Heinz Hartmann, and Ernst Kris, was eventually taken over by Ruth S. Eissler, Anna Freud, Heinz Hartmann, and Marianne Kris. These editors have maintained an unbroken record of excellence in the successive volumes of the Study.

The first article to appear in this annual, "The Genetic Approach in Psychoanalysis," by Heinz Hartmann and Ernst Kris, has been followed by one noteworthy contribution after another to genetic psychology, ego psychology, child development, metapsychology, child analysis, and so forth. A perusal of these papers quickly reveals the extraordinary extent to which they have enriched the whole field of psychoanalysis, beyond the confines of child analysis. The fundamental essays by Anna Freud, René A. Spitz, Margaret S. Mahler, and Edith Jacobson on early psychic development, and the collaborative and individual ones by Heinz Hartmann, Ernst Kris, and Rudolph M. Loewenstein on metapsychology have been seminal contributions to psychoanalysis which will undoubtedly continue to influence and stimulate psychoanalytic thinking for many years to come.

We of the Journal are only too aware of the labor, dedication, and devotion necessary to sustain a scientific publication at so high a level of performance. It is therefore with profound admiration that we offer our heartfelt birthday wishes to an elder sibling for the continuation of its flourishing state of health.

850

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sensory Determinants of the Symbol Three

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
444 Middle Neck Road
Great Neck, New York

Jules Glenn  

SUMMARY

1. Clinical and cultural materials have demonstrated that three is a symbol of the male genitals. In this case the male genital is considered a unit consisting of three parts: the penis and testicles.

2. Different factors that determined this symbolism in a particular patient are discussed. (a) The penis and testicles are in close proximity resulting in the visual perception of a "good Gestalt." (b) The intellectual knowledge that the penis and testicles are anatomically and functionally related leads one to consider them as a unit. (c) My emphasis has been on the role of the similarity of perceptive changes occurring simultaneously in the penis and testicles during sexual excitement in determining the development of the symbol three. I have demonstrated how similar changes take place in penis and testicles—both rise and fall; both become congested and swollen. I have demonstrated that these changes can be perceived through the visceral and somatic sensory nervous systems

as similar. The emphasis on visceral sensations supplements previous observations on the role of visual and kinesthetic perception in the development of symbols.

3. The clinical material leads one to postulate that the visceral basis for the symbol three may be important not only in this particular patient, but may be universal in men. I have postulated a genetic basis for the symbol three. I suggest that there is a period when penis and testicles are both highly cathected and that it is then that the groundwork for this symbol is laid. Perception of changes in the penis and testicles forms the basis for the mental representation of the genitals. Symbolism occurs through projection of the representation of the part of the body as well as through displacement of cathexis.

4. I have also demonstrated the role of specific visceral sensations in the development of psychological symptoms. The fear of explosions was seen to be a fear of projected visceral sensations. The exploding sensations in the testicles as well as the penis were discussed and the bodily changes that account for these feelings described.

[[RunningHead]]

Comments on Libidinal Organizations and Child Development

Lili Peller

Lili Peller

Lili E. Peller
164 East 81 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Lili Peller  

SUMMARY

In this brief survey of Freud's earlier and later concepts of psychosexual development, a good deal has had to be omitted and some things have been oversimplified. The main endeavor was to compare Freud's early and his later conceptualizations of psychosexual development and to relate libidinal development to the unfolding of some adaptive functions. Freud (9) speaks of the principle of the exemplary function of sexuality, i.e., "the principle that sexuality lays down the pattern of behavior." To a certain extent, ego and superego functions and the forces of the id exert a mutual influence upon one another; yet drive developments prompt and dominate the unfolding of all the other functions. Anna Freud (7) expressed it clearly: "Analytic psychology … ascribes to the innate instincts the main role in shaping the personality. It is the claim of the instinctive urges on the mind which results in the development of new functions, the so-called ego-function."

Academic psychology tends to overrate the importance of adaptive functions (motor and cognitive development), to underrate emotional developments, and to ignore the instinctual drives. The result is a view of human development that is shallow and incomplete and thus misleading. If this is done, the child's development appears primarily as the result of the maturation of physiological equipment and of environmental pressures. Psychoanalysis relates the radical changes observable in early childhood above all to the drives, and thus reveals the range, the complexity, and the depth of the metamorphoses that a child undergoes during his formative years.

At the beginning, Freud had to establish the very fact of infantile sexuality and the developmental changes of the zones most responsive to sexual excitation. Later, he stressed the far-reaching ramifications of sexuality in the child's emotional and fantasy life as well as in his efforts to understand what goes on around him. Freud's discovery of psychosexual stages provides the framework for a picture of childhood that is plausible and that has room for both old and very recent observations.

[[RunningHead]]

Loss of Identity and Acting out

Klaus Angel M.D.

Klaus Angel

Klaus Angel, M.D.
12 West 96 Street
New York, N.Y. 10025

Klaus Angel  

SUMMARY

A case of acting out in a borderline patient is reported to demonstrate that, in addition to all the other well-known factors contributing to this phenomenon, preservation of identity is an important element. In borderline cases, where fear of loss of identity represents an ever-present danger, acting out may be resorted to as a derivative of that phase in which locomotion was brought into

83

the service of supporting separation from the mother, and where the gratification of action led to the experiencing of self as an autonomous person.

I cannot refrain from mentioning that if the ideas presented here are correct, it would appear that the Cartesian doctrine "I think, therefore I am" does not tell the entire story. Early in life, it seems this should read: "I act, therefore I am," and only after considerable development does it change to "I think, therefore I am."

[[RunningHead]]

Incomplete Psychoanalytic Training

Sidney Levin M.D. and Joseph Michaels M.D.

Joseph Michaels

Sidney Levin, M.D.
Joseph J. Michaels, M.D.
1265 Beacon Street
Brookline, Mass. 02146

Joseph Michaels  

WITH THE expansion of psychoanalytic education in recent years, increasing attention has been called to the number of candidates who do not complete their training. Such training is a highly complex process and is especially unique in view of the fact that the student has to become an instrument for the treatment process as well as to learn to perform a number of specific functions. As Lewin and Ross (20) have pointed out, "the institutes are unavoidably trying to exert two effects upon the student: to 'educate' him and to 'cure' or 'change' him. Hence the student as a phenomenon fits into two conceptual frameworks: he is the pedagogic unit or object of teaching and the therapeutic object or object of psychoanalytic procedure." They refer to this dual framework as "syncretism."

In view of the relative youthfulness of psychoanalytic education, institutes throughout the country have differed from each other in many respects, such as philosophy and type of training, even though there have been many common denominators (20). This divergence can be contrasted to the more or less standardized curriculum which has developed in medical schools, due largely to the work of Abraham Flexner (7). responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contributions of Longitudinal Studies to Psychoanalytic Theory

Roy Schafer Ph.D.

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer  

In his introduction Peter Neubauer pointed out three salient characteristics of longitudinal studies: they concern a significant period of the developmental process; they refer to propositions in the psychoanalytic theory of development; and they are ongoing and prospectively oriented. Much of the psychoanalytic theory of development has been formulated on the basis of retrospection and reconstruction, that is, on the basis of the grown patient's looking backward. Child analysis, however, qualifies as a longitudinal study in terms of the three characteristics mentioned. Also, the interpretative steps in analysis assume a future developmental organization and thus have predictive meanings. Noting Hartmann's emphasis on the importance of understanding underlying developmental processes, and also Kris's stress on distinguishing validations in analysis from validations outside of it, Neubauer went on to recognize that methods other than the clinical psychoanalytic one are necessary to study the period of early childhood. Thus, the study of certain structural and maturational aspects of the early ego requires assistance from other disciplines and other methods. He observed, however, that in some studies direct observation of children has been carried out in conjunction with subsequent or concurrent psychoanalytic treatment, and also that second analyses have made possible follow-up studies of earlier developmental propositions: such data make it possible to compare propositions based on different fields of observation. As the field of psychoanalytic observation expands into infancy, and as it resorts to observational techniques other than treatment, it also becomes necessary to face the question of how to conceptualize longitudinal data: in terms of clinical theory, developmental theory, general psychological theory, or the higher levels of abstraction of psychoanalytic metapsychology? We must clarify how the propositions of each longitudinal study are related to specific areas of theory formation.

[[RunningHead]]

Historical Notes Concerning Psychoanalysis and Early Language Development

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein, Ph.D.
Reiss-Davis Clinic for Child Guidance
9760 20. Pico Blvd.
Los Angeles 35, Calif.

Rudolf Ekstein  

SUMMARY

The Freudian model of 1895, developed still within the context of neurophysiological considerations, concerning the development of speech, the means of "Verständigung," out of the early helplessness of the infant, indeed has turned out to be a useful first blueprint allowing generations of workers in this field to fill in the details. The second phase consisted of speculative hypotheses concerning the origin of language, influenced very much by the then available theories of psychosexual development. The third phase utilized reconstructions from the analysis of adults and children and stressed the early mother-child relationship in terms of needs to be met. The fourth phase is under the growing dominance of ego psychology, the adaptive point of view, and notions of differentiations of functions. Considerations of speech mechanics, and often too literal assumptions about the origin of speech elements, give way to more sophisticated models concerning the origin and development of speech. The reconstructions of early preverbal genetic facts are seen as new blueprints for investigations and as leading toward a refinement of theory. The fifth contribution comes from direct observations under experimental conditions influenced by the analyst's growing readiness to collaborate with and to learn from other behavioral scientists. A sixth source seems to be the study of language in schizophrenia and related disorders, particularly as they occur in childhood; they permit an improved model of thought and speech development which allows us to see

728

Freud's early and dramatic germinal insight of 1895 as one which has borne rich fruits and which invites new work in the same direction.

Loneliness and Compulsion

Vivian Jarvis

Vivian Jarvis

Vivian Jarvis
89 South Ocean Ave.
Freeport, N.Y.

Vivian Jarvis  

SUMMARY

This paper has tried to show a rather common mother-child relationship marked by too great a maternal emotional withdrawal from the child even when she is with him physically. In such a situation the child will produce, via the path laid down by the repetition compulsion, compulsive manifestations. The compulsive activity symbolized repetitively the wished-for possession of the mother and helped ward off the threatening sorrow due to loneliness which the child has already experienced overwhelmingly. It

157

acts as both defense and gratification and becomes most prominent in the individuation-separation (anal-phallic) period of the child when he becomes aware of himself as a separately functioning psychological entity with special interest in his genital. The mother's withdrawal is occasioned by her unconscious equating of the child with her fantasied infantile phallus damaged by masturbation. The withdrawn relationship, punctuated by sporadic interruptions of closeness, appears to be a re-enactment of the mother's infantile struggle against masturbation with occasional yielding to temptation. The mother, intolerant of the child's phallic impulses, functions most comfortably with him on the sadomasochistic level thus reinforcing his fixation to anality.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Latency

Ted Becker M.D.

Ted Becker

Ted Becker  

Grace Abbate stated that it was her intention to do away with the concept that there was a quiescent period in childhood called latency. Normally not all the sexual energy goes underground. Very complicated psychic constructions and reconstructions go on during this period. The latency child who comes into treatment will be chronologically but not psychologically in latency. In treatment we watch him move into latency.

Elizabeth B. Kaplan presented a paper on "Reflections Regarding Psychomotor Activities During the Latency Period." She described the child in the early latency period as rushing, tumbling, balancing, climbing, sliding—displaying the joy of motor activity; a child involved in rhythmical, chanting, repetitive games and sports which foster peer relationships and which, if interfered with, give us a child who is "out of it," one with marred object relationships; a child idealizing adults of action—ball players, the active mother, firemen, etc. Her emphasis was on the repetitive, rhythmic activities in the normal early latency child which are transient for this period, as distinguished from pathological actions which have permanency and rigidity, such as tics, hyperkinetic and dyskinetic behavior. Besides aiding peer relationships and thus object relationships, these activities in the maturing organism play a part in the development of ego functions—writing, carpentry, learning to throw a ball, batting, sewing, etc. Special emphasis is put on the role these activities play in helping the latency child deal with his main task, the warding off of masturbation and incestuous fantasies.

Presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1964. Chairman: Grace McLean Abbate, M.D.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Genetic Determinants of Obsessive-Compulsive Phenomena in Character Formation

Sigmund Gabe M.D.

Sigmund Gabe

Sigmund Gabe  

Arthur F. Valenstein explained that the panel hoped to concentrate on those aspects of ego development which manifest themselves by certain obsessive or compulsivelike features of behavior in childhood, perhaps before the anal phase and sometimes as early as the point when the preverbal period gives way to beginning speech. The term "genetic determinants" underscored the fact that the panel would approach the topic from the viewpoint of the causal antecedents, touching on the issue of the complementary series of heredity and environment. Genetics is the branch of biology which is concerned with heredity and constitutional variations, and which studies the make-up of an organism as it evolves out of the genes. On the other hand, when we seek to make a genetic reconstruction in analysis, we only dimly, if at all, mean the actual genic factors—although we acknowledge their existence. We refer rather to an explanatory hypothesis in terms of origin, development, and causal antecedents in the broader sense. And assuredly these points of view are not mutually exclusive. Biologists admit that there is no such thing as a purely genetic determinant of development uninfluenced by environment, while the importance of constitutional factors was acknowledged in analysis from the very beginning. However, the issue of the role of constitutional factors in character development and in neurosogenesis did not really become central until Freud published "Analysis Terminable and Interminable," with its emphasis on the innate variations in the strength of the instincts and in the constitution of the ego.

The panel would take up the question whether there is something particular in character development that antedates conflict and remains outside of it, as well as the modifiable and unmodifiable modalities of character through analysis.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rossini—A Psychoanalytic Approach to "The Great Renunciation"

Daniel Schwartz M.D.

Daniel Schwartz

Daniel W. Schwartz, M.D.
295 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10024

Daniel Schwartz  

SUMMARY

Gioacchino Rossini, when only thirty-seven years old and at the pinnacle of success, permanently ceased his career as an opera composer. A psychoanalytic approach to "the great renunciation"—unique in the annals of serious music and heretofore only inadequately explained—suggests that the crucial factor was the death of the composer's mother two years previously. From his earliest years, which were punctuated by frequent separations from her, he had never established a secure, maturely neutralized, and internalized image of his mother which could lead to the formation of a stable ego structure and a meaningful sense of identity. Throughout his life, Rossini continued to act out his infantile and

568

highly ambivalent attachment to her; operatic composition itself was a precariously sublimated and insufficiently neutralized expression of his aggression.3 This intense ambivalence toward his mother made it impossible for him to mourn her in a healthy way, and his refusal to compose was the ultimate expression of his unconscious anger at her for having deserted him. Most of the remaining thirty-nine years of Rossini's life were basically one long agitated depression, which at times reached psychotic proportions. Only when he had established sufficient obsessional defenses and a stable enough dependent, sadomasochistic relationship with his second wife (and perhaps, too, when the instinctual energies involved had sufficiently diminished in intensity with old age) could he compose music again, albeit still not opera; but even then he seems to have had some awareness of the aggression involved, for he called these compositions "sins" and, by negation, identified himself with Judas. While he had always consciously maintained an exaggerated adoration of his mother, it was in attempting to master the unconscious aggression toward her that he had originally written such beautiful music; and it was his inability to neutralize this aggression sufficiently that, unfortunately, led to "the great renunciation."

Ambiguity and Human Imperfection

Sidney Tarachow M.D.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow, M.D.
123 East 80 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Sidney Tarachow  

SUMMARY

The thesis of this paper is that we need periodic relief from the burdens of certain ego functions, notably synthesis and fusion of ambivalent tendencies. Various social and psychological institutions or acts of creativity which afford such relief are reviewed. Creative art is a human product which establishes contact with the wide range of ambiguous feelings which we are in danger of losing in the process of maturation and civilization. The very ambiguity and imprecision of art contributes to its safety as a long-lasting device. Art thus enlarges the ego by increasing its points of reference to the unconscious infantile and ambivalent tendencies. Comedy serves a similar function by helping us re-experience the paradoxes of childishness and rebellion alongside of maturity. Even religious systems must offer outlets for aggression as well as love. For happiness and mental health childishness and ambiguity must not be too thoroughly sacrificed in the interests of rationality.

[[RunningHead]]

Dream Interpretation and the Psychology of Dreaming

Richard Jones Ph.D.

Richard Jones

Richard M. Jones, Ph.D.
Department of Psychology Brandeis University
Waltham, Mass. 02154

Richard Jones  

SUMMARY

The psychoanalytic theory of dream interpretation and the psychoanalytic theory of dreaming are differentiated in terms of their respective achievements, conceptual properties, and implications for research. Four specific lines of contrast are drawn between these two approaches to the study of dreams: (1) their social significances, (2) their immediate purposes, (3) their criteria of validity, and (4) their relations to each other. Lack of clarity on these points is

318

shown to lead in dream psychology to reifying the interpretive concept of "latent content," and to overgeneralizing the interpretive principle of "dream distortion"; and in dream interpretation, to misapplying the wish-fulfillment hypothesis. The care with which Freud sought to maintain these distinctions, and some instances in which he failed to do so, are noted throughout. The tendency to overlook the distinctions is seen to stem from Freud's commitment to perfecting an improved method of dream interpretation while simultaneously laying the foundation for a psychology of dreaming. The proper relation of all methods of dream interpretation to dream psychology is judged to be purely heuristic.

[[RunningHead]]

Scientific Proceedings—panel Reports—The Relationship Between Child Analysis and the Theory and Practice of Adult Psychoanalysis

Gabriel Casuso M.D.

Gabriel Casuso

Gabriel Casuso  

Leo Rangell opened the meeting by pointing to the vexing administrative problems which have lately been present in the relationships between child analysis and adult analysis. He expressed the opinion that this panel had the opportunity and luxury to explore the scientific issues involved and that we might well expect one to clarify the other. The scientific considerations can be encompassed under two categories, i.e., the theoretical and technical standpoints.

From the technical standpoint, there is no doubt that many factors, such as differences in the levels of psychic organizations, the lack of a well-developed observing and critical ego, and many other internal psychic factors, require considerable differences in the methods of eliciting unconscious material as well as the use to which they are put. Nevertheless Rangell supports the opinion that the essence of the psychoanalytic method can be and is retained in child analysis in common with adult analysis. However, some child analysts make the mistake of ignoring the differences that exist and artificially squeeze the child patient into the method which is standard for the adult. Rangell also pointed out that within child analysis itself, there is as much difference between child analysis and child therapy as there is in the case of adults between analysis and analytic psychotherapy.

From the theoretical standpoint, child and adult analysis are part of one continuum of understanding, with the same metapsychological body of facts uniting them.

Presented at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Los Angeles, May, 1964. Chairman: Leo Rangell, M.D.

Working Through and Termination of Analysis

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein, Ph.D.
9760 W. Pico Boulevard
Los Angeles 35, Calif.

Rudolf Ekstein  

SUMMARY

In this communication I have touched upon the technical issues which are described in the literature. I have stressed our growing sophistication concerning the difficulties of termination, concerning the structural as well as technical modifications which have been discussed and which have replaced the early optimism. Rather than contributing to the technology of termination I have attempted to focus on its philosophy. In order to do this, I have used the analogy of the ending of a play, the function of its epilogue, in order to lead us to a model, a prototype of the total process of change, so that the working-through function of the process of termination could be seen against that background. I have synthesized the primary model of thinking with the secondary model of thinking in order to indicate certain features of the process of working through, which if successful moves from the compulsion to repeat to spontaneous repetition in the service of adaptation, rather than in the service of the neurotic conflict. The adaptive function of the afterplay, the epilogue, has been discussed in terms of remembering the task completed; recollection then was seen in the service of reconstruction, of mastery, of preparation for future tasks. The epilogue was shown to become a prologue. I have touched on the special difficulties of this termination procedure in work with children and in training analysis, and I have put emphasis on the fact that both situations create special technical problems including countertransference problems because of the difficulty to separate the therapeutic process from the total educational or training task. Termination of analysis is seen as an important aspect of working through, as a process rather than as an arbitrary or ideal ending point.

[[RunningHead]]

The Defensive Function of an Altered State of Consciousness—A Hypnoid State

Robert Dickes M.D.

Robert Dickes

Robert Dickes, M.D.
451 Clarkson Avenue
Brooklyn, New York

Robert Dickes  

SUMMARY

Certain phenomena, commonplace in our culture, and which are usually termed fatigue, drowsiness, or sleepiness are actually manifestations of a hypnoid state. This is a special alteration in the state of consciousness which should be distinguished from other alterations in consciousness such as hypnagogic phenomena, fugue states, and others. The hypnoid state is not uncommon during an analytic hour and may be noted in many other life situations as well. These are situations which involve the patient in struggles with unconscious unacceptable sexual and aggressive impulses related to repressed memories. Several illustrations as observed in my patients are presented. The severest cases were exposed to major trauma in childhood during which time the hypnoid state was utilized defensively.

The hypnoid state is classified as an ego defense and categorized as another in the ego's repertory of defenses. The hypnoid state, as is typical for a defense, is reported as protecting the ego

399

against unacceptable instinctual demands. A theoretical discussion of the hypnoid state includes the consideration of classical hypnosis and sleep. Points of similarity and difference are noted.

Clinically, the hypnoid state can be noted to range from the mildest, almost imperceptible interference with normal alertness to a deep sleeplike state. A few of my patients report that the minimal type of alteration in alertness has been present in them for considerable lengths of time.

This special alteration in the normal state of alertness can be noted in other cultures as well as in our own. The Balinese culture is one in which hypnoid phenomena are acceptable manifestations of behavior. Similar alterations in consciousness may be observed in shamans and other religious devotees such as Buddhist monks.

Shakespeare's Othello contains a typical example of a hypnoid response to aggression. The phenomenon which I have termed the Desdemona phenomenon is exemplified by Desdemona's response that she is "half-asleep" following Othello's aggressive declamation.

The hypnoid state under consideration is so ubiquitous that it is difficult to classify all such reactions as necessarily pathological. In analysis, it constitutes a serious resistance. It may also be noted as a countertransference manifestation and must be combatted.

On Time and Timelessness

Shelley Orgel M.D.

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel, M.D.
152 East Avenue
Norwalk, Conn.

Shelley Orgel  

SUMMARY

Problems related to the uses and meanings of time to a young woman who suffered from chronic lateness have been discussed. The time difficulties have been traced to the unpredictable alternation of overstimulation and abandonment in the early relationship to her mother. By denying the impingment of time, she could regressively revive fantasies of fusion with the mother of the first oral stage, and simultaneously ward off repetition of early separation traumata and intense oral-sadistic instinctual representations. The resultant ego distortions, especially in object relationships, and as reflected in the analytic transference, have been examined.

[[RunningHead]]

The Goals of Psychoanalysis—A Survey of Analytic Viewpoints

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Robert Wallerstein  

SUMMARY

In this broad survey of the diversity of analytic viewpoints on the goals of analysis, I have organized the presentation along three major dimensions, each of them posing an apparent opposition—or complementarity—in emphasis. The first has been the distinction between goallessness as the procedural stance necessary to technical analytic work and the far-reaching goal of fundamental character realignment as the outcome toward which the analytic work points. The second has been the distinction between the outcome goals of analytic therapy conceptualized in terms of observable behavior and relationship changes and the process goals of analytic therapy conceptualized in metapsychological explanatory terms that posit at least implicitly a theory of therapy, of how analysis brings about change and reaches its outcome goals. The third has been the oscillating distinction between the more limited and the more ambitious, the more pessimistic and the more optimistic view of how much of this sought-for change can actually be accomplished within the limits of human analytic endeavor. I feel that this totality and this complexity of considerations, inevitably entering into meaningful discussion of the goals of analysis, need to be set as part of the framework for useful examination of the theoretical and the technical limitations of analysis in bringing about the attainment of its goals.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on Countertransference

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg, M.D.
The Menninger Clinic
Topeka, Kansas

Otto Kernberg  

SUMMARY

Contrasting views of countertransference and its clinical use are outlined. It is suggested that countertransference may be helpful in evaluating the degree of regression in the patient and in clarifying the transference paradigms during severe regression.

Patients with the potential for severe regression in analysis or expressive psychotherapy tend to foster serious countertransference complications, especially "counteridentification."

It is suggested that countertransference complications in the form of "counteridentification" are related to the partial reactivation of early ego identifications and early defensive mechanisms in the analyst. While these counteridentifications may be the source of important information about the analytic situation, they are also a serious threat to the analysis, and predispose the analyst to the development of "chronic countertransference fixation."

The following signs of chronic countertransference fixation are described: reappearance of abandoned neurotic character traits of the analyst in his interactions with a particular patient; "emotional discontinuation" of the analysis; unrealistic "total dedication"; "micro-paranoid" attitudes toward the patient. These countertransference complications present themselves especially in the treatment of patients with a potential for severe regression and whose conflicts center around pregenital aggression.

The importance of "concern" as a general trait of the analyst, helpful in protecting him from the countertransference complications mentioned, is described. Some of the characteristics, preconditions, and realistic limitations of the development of concern are mentioned.

[[RunningHead]]

Modes of Insight

Paul Myerson M.D.

Paul Myerson

Paul Graves Myerson, M.D.
171 Harrison Avenut
Boston, Mass. 02111

Paul Myerson  

SUMMARY

Two episodes of insight occurring toward the end of a patient's psychoanalysis are described and their differences emphasized for purposes of exposition. One is characterized as psychoanalytic insight and the other as reality-oriented insight. It is recognized that both types are essential for working through. In each type there are various phases or stages which become integrated into a Gestalt through the psychoanalytic process. The various phases are delineated individually in some detail, and speculations are made about the genetic roots of each phase. It is felt that a capacity to suspend attention, to recapitulate in fantasy earlier efforts at mastery, to observe such efforts in an autonomous manner, to re-experience qualities of the mental state prominent during this attempt at mastery, and finally to reintegrate through a deep acceptance of

791

one's own reactions are crucial features of psychoanalytic insight. This contrasts with reality-oriented insight where there is a different mode of deploying attention, where attempted mastery through fantasy is not a prominent aspect of the process, and where self-observation is largely in terms of a better understanding of one's relationship with key figures. This type of insight is nonetheless of a high order of integration and may have considerable adaptive value.

The genetic factors which foster a potentiality for psychoanalytic insight are discussed in detail. While these factors are complex, the interaction of the child and his parents, as he attempts to find a solution for conflictual situations, particularly as the parents encourage the child to tolerate uncertainty and to master his difficulties in an integrated manner, are of paramount importance in the genesis of this process.

Psychoanalytic Implications of Recent Research on Sleep and Dreaming—Part II: Implications for Psychoanalytic Theory

Charles Fisher M.D.

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher, M.D.
The Mount Sinai Hospital
100 Street and Fifth Ave.
New York, N. Y. 10029

Charles Fisher  

The Relationship of Instinctual Drives to Physiological Processes

IN PART I of this paper (11), I have presented an extensive summary of much of the experimental work on sleep and dreaming of the past decade. Although it is impressive how well Freud's theory of dreaming can encompass many of the new empirical findings, it is obvious that some modification of theory is necessary. The most general statement that Freud made about the function of dreaming is taken from his last published comments about dreams (18): "We shall be taking all our observations into account if we say that every dream is an attempt to put aside a disturbance of sleep by means of wish-fulfillment. The dream is thus the guardian of sleep. This attempt can be more or less completely successful; it can also fail—in which case the sleeper wakes up, apparently aroused by the dream itself" (p. 57). Freud included four categories of stimuli or excitations as potential disturbers of sleep: (i) most important were the repressed wishes in the system Ucs., in the older terminology; the instinctual drives of the id pressing for discharge, in the new structural theory; (ii) preconscious day residues; (iii) stimuli impinging on the sense organs from the external world; (iv) internal somatic stimuli such as those arising from hunger, thirst, a full bladder, etc.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Dostoevsky

Fritz Schmidl

Fritz Schmidl

Fritz Schmidl
6036 Upland Terrace South
Seattle 18, Wash.

Fritz Schmidl  

SUMMARY

While, in his essay on "Dostoevsky and Parricide" Freud extolled Dostoevsky, the author, saying that "his place is not far behind Shakespeare," he dealt in an extremely critical manner with Dostoevsky, the man. He even stated: "The future of human civilization

530

will have little to thank him for." Freud's tentative diagnosis of Dostoevsky's epilepsy as not organic but affective is open to doubt. An inquiry into the possible reasons for Freud's intolerance in dealing with Dostoevsky's character shows that there were three apparently insurmountable differences in the Weltanschauung of the two great men: (1) Freud the determinist could not accept Dostoevsky's indeterminism. (2) Dostoevsky's belief in religion as the basis of morality ran counter to Freud's deepest conviction. (3) The rationalist Freud could not stand Dostoevsky's religious mysticism. It seems like an irony of history that Freud was asked to write about Dostoevsky at about the time when he was working on The Future of an Illusion. While Freud's writings about religion during the first two decades of psychoanalysis were more or less objective psychological studies, he himself, in a letter to Pfister, called The Future of an Illusion a "declaration of war." It also seems that a change took place in Freud's attitude toward moral systems. In letters written in 1915 and in 1918 Freud identified himself with Th. Vischer's dictum: "What is moral is self-evident." In his letter to Theodor Reik of April 14, 1929, Freud stated a belief in "a scientifically objective social assessment of ethics." The changes of Freud's attitude toward religion and systems of morality are likely to have contributed to his condemnation of Dostoevsky, the man, in 1928.

responseSet.title: Current Status of the Theory of the Superego
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Current Status of the Theory of the Superego

Stanley Goodman M.D.

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman  

Martin Stein introduced the panel by stating the urgent reasons for a reevaluation of the concept of the "superego" at this time. He said that we are not yet entirely clear about considering the superego a specialized part of the ego, or a group of functions so modified as to be, conceptually at least, on a level with id and ego. The dimensions of the superego shrink in both clinical and theoretical discussions as its individual functions are subsumed under other structures—usually the ego. However, we would be most reluctant to conceive of the healthy adult as being able to dispense with superego, much less id and ego functions, although in time we might dispense with, or greatly modify, these concepts. We are faced with a dilemma. On the one hand, we possess concepts of the superego derived by logical analysis from the postulates and hypotheses of metapsychology; on the other, we encounter concepts of superego function derived from clinical observation. The theoretical formulations are relatively precise but reveal their inadequacies when we attempt to apply them broadly to clinical data. The clinical formulations are working hypotheses that do cover a wide range of human behavior but still retain a number of internal inconsistencies. We must realize, therefore, that we do not always deal with data on the same level of abstraction, and that we may use different theoretical and clinical formulations, which nevertheless employ the same terms, to our everlasting confusion—unless we know what level we are on.

Manuel Furer presented a brief survey of the concept of the superego in psychoanalytic literature. The first full discussion of the genesis and contents of what Freud later called the superego was in the monograph Totem and Taboo (1913). The argument there was that the source of conscience, a manifestation of consciousness but internalized and independent, is emotional ambivalence, the conflict between the hatred and the love for the father.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Implications of Recent Research on Sleep and Dreaming—Part I: Empirical Findings

Charles Fisher M.D.

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher, M.D.
The Mount Sinai Hospital
100 Street and Fifth Ave
New York, N.Y. 100293

Charles Fisher  

FREUD'S earliest program was to develop a general psychology on neurophysiological lines (53). Although this attempt failed and Freud concluded that the theory of behavior must be a psychological one, he never gave up the belief that ultimately the link between psychoanalysis and physico-chemico-biological processes would be found. During the past decade some revolutionary discoveries have been made on the nature of sleep and dreaming, the full implications of which have not yet been integrated into psychoanalytic theory. These discoveries involve significant correlations between dreaming as a psychological process and the physiological and neurophysiological events that are concomitant and synchronous with it. They constitute what Snyder (150) has called "The New Biology of Dreaming." Most of what we know about dreams and dreaming we have learned from Freud, but it must be admitted that not many basic advances in our understanding have occurred since Freud's time. As revealing as the psychoanalytic method has been, the answers to many simple questions are beyond its investigative reach. How much dreaming occurs each night? When during the night does it occur? In what stage of sleep? Are there age and sex differences? Are there fluctuations in the amount of dreaming in various types of mental disease? Can drugs affect the nature and quantity of dreaming?

[[RunningHead]]

Memory and Repression

William Niederland M.D.

William Niederland

William Niederland  

During one of the early debates of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society which dealt with a then famous book by Moebius,1 Freud observed that "the topic of memory is far from being exhausted… The whole problem of remembering and forgetting has not yet been dealt with, neither by myself nor by anyone else."2

Though these words, spoken in 1907, were not expressly stated during the proceedings to be reported, they may well serve as a useful point of departure, if not comparison, with regard to a consideration of the theoretical and clinical viewpoints set forth in the panel discussion. In fact, Charles Brenner mentioned in his introductory remarks that one of the purposes of this meeting was the clarification of our concepts of mental processes which are customarily subsumed under the terms of "memory," "remembering," "repression," and "forgetting." Brenner observed that practicing analysts in their daily work were usually more familiar or at least more concerned with the phenomenon of repression than with that of memory per se. He focused attention on what he called "mental memory or psychological memory" in contrast to various theories of memory as expounded in biological or biochemical studies and examined in experimental research projects of a prevailingly chemical nature. The analyst's interest in psychological memory processes is essentially based on the theoretical and clinical considerations derived from his daily work with patients.

In the first formal paper, entitled "The Theory of Repression," Charles Brenner presented a conceptualization of the theory of repression which, according to him, differed in some respects from the customary approach to the subject. He observed that from the early Freudian formulations—which

Darwin as the Source of Freud's Neo-Lamarckianism

Lucille Ritvo

Lucille Ritvo

Lucille B. Ritvo, M.A.
Race Brook Road
Woodbridge, Conn.

Lucille Ritvo  

SUMMARY

Historical study reveals that Darwin rather than Lamarck is the more likely source of Freud's neo-Lamarckianism. Freud's single scientific reference to Lamarck is in his paper "On Coca," in which he cites a 1783 paper by Lamarck on the same subject. Lamarck's evolutionary ideas, in contrast to the invertebrate classification for which he was justly famous, had been ignored or scorned in the

513

period of Freud's education, until Haeckel sought precursors for Darwin. Freud's interest in Lamarck appears about World War I when he proposed to Ferenczi a joint work on Lamarckianism and psychoanalysis. But nothing came of it.

Freud's interest in and knowledge of Darwin, on the other hand, extend from his high school days through his preanalytic work in biology to Moses and Monotheism. He owned Darwin's The Descent of Man, which he considered one of the "ten most significant books." He referred to it in his analytic writings and also to Darwin's The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals and to The Variation of Animals and Plants under Domestication. He classed Darwin with Copernicus and referred to him as "the great Darwin." Freud also used "strict Darwinian lines of thought."

Nevertheless, from 1913 onward Freud's writings contain neo-Lamarckian statements about the inheritance of acquired characteristics, particularly in relation to the oedipus complex, which Freud maintained even against the protests of Jones and the regrets of Kris. This paper presented the thesis that Freud's use of and attitude toward the inheritance of acquired characteristics were influenced by his knowledge of Darwin's writings and the vicissitudes of Darwin's theory of evolution during Freud's lifetime. Darwin was more and more forced to acknowledge neo-Lamarckian mechanisms by the serious scientific problems with which biologists and physicists assailed his theory of evolution by natural selection. Mendel's work remained unknown until 1900; and in the decade that followed its rediscovery, Morgan himself was skeptical about natural selection until his own work on Drosophila revealed the discrete physical basis of heredity which he called the chromosome. The blending theory of inheritance of Darwin's day made evolution by natural selection impossible because the chance variations would necessarily be swamped under by the majority characteristics. Simultaneously, Lord Kelvin launched an attack which was unanswerable until the discovery of radioactivity and Rutherford's announcement in 1904 of a source of heat within the earth itself. The view of Helmholtz and Kelvin that the sun was an incandescent liquid mass dissipating its energy at a rapid pace had enabled Lord Kelvin to demonstrate mathematically, in terms of

514

heat loss, that the earth's crust could not have maintained its stability over the enormous time range required by Darwin's evolutionary theory.

Freud's interest in Lamarck appears in the decade after the answers were found to these two serious scientific objections to Darwin's theory. Darwin, except in the first edition of his On the Origin of Species, which was quickly sold out and which was not translated into German, included more and more Lamarckian statements in his subsequent writings and, therefore, in the works which Freud knew. By 1914 biologists might maintain the exclusivity of the mechanism of natural selection, but they could waiver when a new discovery such as hormones hinted at the possibility of a means for the inheritance of acquired characteristics. And in 1923 at least one geologist, C. C. Fagg, in his Presidential Address to the Croydon Natural History and Scientific Society, maintained that the exclusive acceptance of natural selection was neo-Darwinism and a perversion of Darwin's thinking, of which he doubted Darwin would approve. Freud's interest in Lamarck appears when the attack is no longer against evolution itself but when the battle lines become drawn between evolution by natural selection and evolution by acquired characteristics, with the names of Darwin and Lamarck being assigned to each. It was only in the last decade of Freud's life that the accumulating data of genetics and ecology ended controversy among biologists. The Darwin Freud had known was not only what is today labeled Darwinian but was also what is now called Lamarckian or neo-Lamarckian. Therefore, for Freud there was no inconsistency in lauding the "great Darwin" and at the same time utilizing the inheritance of acquired characteristics where it suited him.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Notes on the History of Morality

David Beres M.D.

David Beres

David Beres, M.D.
151 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10023

David Beres  

THE APPLICATION of psychoanalytic concepts to history is a hazardous undertaking, the more so if the area of investigation is not that of a historical character or a historical period but a whole trend in human development. I must share with you my qualms about presenting to you the topic I have chosen. It is one to which a lifetime should be devoted. I take courage from a statement made by William Langer in a presidential address before the American Historical Association (32), to the effect that there is urgent need for deepening historical understanding "through the exploitation of the concepts and findings of modern psychology," by which he refers not to "classical or academic psychology … but rather to psychoanalysis."

My theme is the history of morality. The concern of psychoanalysis with the problem of morality is not to set moral values or to influence the conduct of men but to study how, in the individual, moral values develop and how the moral function manifests itself. Does morality have a history? Has man changed over the centuries or is man unchanged, and are the differences that we note between the behavior and attitudes of man in past centuries and today due to the effects of different external environments? Does man have innate moral attitudes? Does he have an innate moral sense? These are some of the questions that arise in the broad perspective of a historical approach.

I distinguish between ethics and morals.

Presented at the Sunday morning Plenary Session of the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December 6, 1964, New York.

Submitted December, 1964

Observations on a "Natural Experiment": Helen Keller

Hartvig Dahl M.D.

Hartvig Dahl

Hartvig Dahl, M.D.
707 Broadway
New York, N.Y. 10003

Hartvig Dahl  

THE VALIDATION of psychoanalytic theoretical propositions is, according to Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein (4), "a gradual and slow process in which insight derived from many experiences is weighted; in which many sources of evidence have to be taken into account, foremost among them clinical experiences and the study of child development. Under these conditions neatness in theorizing gains its great importance. It becomes imperative at each step to be aware of the point at which a modification [or retention] of theoretical assumptions is required."

While it is true that every clinical experience is, in a sense, a kind of natural experiment, it is nonetheless evident that certain cases more than others favor us with evidence which clearly and directly supports (or is incompatible with) our basic theoretical models. Helen Keller is just such a favorable experiment of nature. The unique facts of Helen Keller's childhood development—the relative sensory isolation from deafness and blindness, the consequent delay in acquiring language and social habits until relative neurological maturity, and the special qualities of her teacher—combine to offer us evidence of a most compelling nature.

I shall limit myself to just a few of the many issues1

Read at meetings of the Seattle Psychoanalytic Society on February 10, 1964 and the American Psychoanalytic Association on May 1, 1964.

Currently at the Research Center for Mental Health, New York University under a Public Health Service fellowship (1-F3-MH-23,108-01) from National Institute of Mental Health.

Submitted May 25, 1964

On the Creation of Beauty and Thought: The Unique as Vicissitude

Joseph Coltrera M.D.

Joseph Coltrera

Joseph T. Coltrera, M.D.
69 Willow Street
Brooklyn Heights
Brooklyn, N.Y. 11201

Joseph Coltrera  

Ideas are the occasions of grace by which history comes to pass. Our own age has come in large part of its special concerns and its commitments out of a unique apposition of especially sympathetic minds in a historical time given cast by the dialogues they effected. Between 1856 and 1877 were born Sigmund Freud, Henri Bergson, Benedetto Croce, Max Weber, and Emile Durkheim; Georges Sorel was born in 1847 and Vilfredo Pareto in 1848. A generation of precursors had come before them: Friedrich Nietzsche, Fyodor Dostoevsky, Karl Marx, Ludwig Feuerbach, and Wilhelm Dilthey. Applied psychoanalysis grew out of and was dedicated to the spirit of the remarkable humanistic dialogue in which these men conceived their particular time in history. To continue in the intent of such dialogue, applied psychoanalysis must address itself to the questions posed by a modern predicament in its own terms. This essay represents an inquiry into certain methodological arrests of the psychoanalytic idea which have compromised the continuance of such dialogue down into our time.

[[RunningHead]]

Limitations of Psychoanalysis

Paul Gray M.D.

Paul Gray

Paul Gray  

Arnold Z. Pfeffer called attention to the fact that this was the first panel of the American Psychoanalytic Association specifically directed to the subject of limitations. Freud's classical paper, "Analysis Terminable and Interminable," is the main contribution to this problem. Pfeffer clarified the scope of the panel by defining some perspectives suggested by the title of the panel. He stated that attention would be concentrated upon the limits of psychoanalysis in achieving therapeutic and intrapsychic change.

While the subject matter might be divided into limitations in patients and those in the analyst, the panel's chief purpose was to explore limitations in the therapeutic and intrapsychic changes brought about by the analytic process. Pfeffer felt that the analytic process lends itself to such consideration only if the entire analytic situation is taken into account, that is, the specific arrangement between patient and analyst which brings about the analytic process. The analytic process may be understood as a function of the interaction between the two—analyst and patient Thus, limitations of the analytic process and hence of therapeutic and intrapsychic change depend upon limitations of the interaction between analyst and patient. Bearing in mind that at one level therapeutic change based on intrapsychic change arises from the resolution of psychic conflict, a focus suggests itself for discussion—limitations of the degree to which conflicts can be resolved.

While Freud's later observations were viewed by some as indicating pessimism regarding the therapeutic possibilities of analysis, Pfeffer preferred to view these observations as realistic rather than pessimistic.

In order to provide a fresh background for the discussion of limitations, Robert S. Wallerstein presented a paper, "The Goals of Psychoanalysis: A Survey of Analytic Viewpoints." This important survey, the only formal presentation at the panel, stimulated much of the day's discussion.

[[RunningHead]]

Conceptions of Superego Development

Steven Hammerman M.D.

Steven Hammerman

Steven Hammerman, M.D.
1942 Lombard Street
Philadelphia, Pa. 19146

Steven Hammerman  

SUMMARY

The superego proper as a discrete internalized psychic structure develops with the resolution of the oedipal conflicts. It is the end product of a prolonged period of psychic growth and development. It is conceived to be a product of the ultimate fusion of the matured ego ideal, various superego precursors, and those secondary identifications within the ego which result from the need to relinquish the oedipal objects. Concepts of the origin, development, functions, maturation, and composition of the ego ideal have been delineated.

A concept of the phase-specific, reciprocal, mutual enhancement of ego and superego maturation and development has been utilized. Concepts of the role of self-individuation, the distinction between primary and secondary identifications, the capacity to establish object cathexis, and the utilization and modification of instinctual energies in the development of the superego proper have been examined. The nature and differentiation of shame and guilt and their genetic relationship to the superego proper have been delineated. Finally, the functioning, regressive potentials as well as the capacity for further growth and alteration of the superego proper during latency, adolescence, and adulthood have been described.
